title: Reuben Sandwich
subtitle: Toasted rye with salt beef, sauerkraut, Swiss cheese, and Thousand Island dressing
description: The Reuben is one of America's great sandwiches: thin slices of salt beef (or corned beef), tangy sauerkraut, and melted Swiss cheese on toasted rye bread, with Thousand Island dressing on both slices. The sandwich is pressed in a hot pan or on a griddle until the bread is golden and the cheese has melted into the filling. Making a good Reuben is about the assembly and the heat — every element must be hot when it comes together.
sourceNotes: The Reuben sandwich has two competing origin stories — one placing it at the Blackstone Hotel in Omaha, Nebraska, around 1925, another at Reuben's Restaurant in New York City. The Nebraska connection is now generally considered more credible. The sandwich won a national sandwich competition in 1956, which popularised it across the United States.

recipeTools.notes: Or a griddle — needs to hold heat evenly and have a flat surface to press the sandwich.
Wide and flat — for pressing the sandwich and flipping it.

glossaryTerms.definition: An American dressing made from mayonnaise, ketchup, and finely chopped pickles and onion. It is the defining condiment of a Reuben sandwich.

ingredients.notes: Thick slices of dark rye — marble or caraway rye also work
Thinly sliced — salt beef or pastrami also work
Drained and squeezed dry — excess liquid will make the bread soggy

steps: Mix together the Thousand Island dressing ingredients.
Squeeze the sauerkraut in a clean cloth or kitchen paper to remove as much liquid as possible.
Butter one side of each bread slice.
Place two slices butter-side down. Spread the Thousand Island dressing on the unbuttered (top) side of all four slices.
On two of the slices, layer the corned beef, sauerkraut, and Swiss cheese. Top with the remaining slices, dressing-side in, butter-side out.
Heat a heavy frying pan over a medium heat. Place the sandwiches in the pan. Press down gently with a spatula.
Cook for 3–4 minutes until the bottom slice is golden and the cheese begins to melt. Flip carefully and cook for 3–4 minutes more until both sides are golden.
Cut in half and serve immediately.

content.text: Squeeze the sauerkraut thoroughly dry before it goes in — wet sauerkraut makes the bread soggy from the inside during cooking. The
should coat both inner faces of the bread; the butter goes on the outside for the toasting. Medium heat is right: too high and the bread burns before the cheese melts.

Mix the Thousand Island dressing. Squeeze {{sauerkraut}} of sauerkraut completely dry. Butter one side of each of {{rye-bread}} of rye bread slices. Spread the dressing on the unbuttered sides of all four slices. Layer {{corned-beef}} of corned beef, the sauerkraut, and {{swiss-cheese}} of Swiss cheese on two slices. Top with the remaining slices (dressing in, butter out). Cook in a heavy pan over medium heat, pressing gently, for 3–4 minutes per side until golden and the cheese has melted. Cut and serve at once.
